text.title: Fail2ban Setup for Ubuntu 24 Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every application of `Fail2ban Setup for Ubuntu 24` must enforce. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: fail2ban sshd jail port MUST match actual sshd port (often 22022, not 22). Mismatched port = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Default jail watches port 22; if sshd runs on 22022 the jail bans no one (no auth attempts on 22) while real attacks slip through.

rule.statement: On Ubuntu 24, jail backend MUST be `nftables` (not iptables-legacy). Wrong backend = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Ubuntu 24 ships nftables-native; iptables-legacy is a compat shim and may not persist bans on reboot.

rule.statement: bantime MUST escalate via `bantime.increment = true` + `bantime.factor`. Flat bantime = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Flat bantime lets persistent attackers come back the same evening; escalating bans (1h → 4h → 1d → 1w) make repeat attacks expensive.

rule.statement: Operator's WireGuard subnet MUST be whitelisted via `ignoreip`. Missing = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Operator running fail2ban locally during VPN reconnect can trip own jail; whitelist prevents self-lockout.

rule.statement: Jail MUST use an action that pings Telegram (or webhook) on ban so operator sees real attack patterns. Silent bans = REJECT for solo ops.
rule.rationale: Silent bans give a false sense of safety; an alert lets the operator notice when patterns shift (e.g. CN ↔ RU swap).

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume cycles and produce false confidence.
